role_summary: Co-organizer of the Howard University Nonviolent Action Group (NAG); Freedom Rider on the Anniston and Montgomery-to-Jackson legs in May 1961; voter-registration worker in Baton Rouge.
ai_reading: Diamond's top semantic neighbors are Harold K. Brown (entry 55, count 111), C. T. Vivian (entry 134, SCLC), Robert L. Carter (entry 108, NAACP LDF lawyer), Joan Trumpauer Mulholland (entry 60, Freedom Rider), and Judge D'Army Bailey (entry 26). The cluster is unusually heterogeneous, mixing SCLC inner circle (Vivian), NAACP legal (Carter), Freedom Rides (Mulholland), and the BPP-adjacent Brown, rather than concentrating in his Howard NAG / SNCC affiliation. The embedding therefore reads his testimony as cross-faction, the closest cultural analog to the May 1961 Freedom Ride alliance that pulled together SNCC, CORE, the NAACP, and SCLC in one operation. His axis positions are all near-zero (the largest is +0.34 toward Collective Discipline), placing him among the most centrist voices in the corpus and consistent with the cross-faction neighbor pattern. The Howard NAG identification that defines his cultural placement is one cluster among several the embedding does NOT foreground, a reading the cultural-historical record would not have predicted.
biographical_paragraph: Dion Diamond was president of Howard University's freshman class in 1959-60 when he and others organized the Nonviolent Action Group (NAG), the campus arm of SNCC that ran sit-ins in Virginia and North Carolina and that, in one stretch, desegregated an area of Alexandria, Virginia inside two weeks [src: 1]. He had been born February 7, 1941 in Petersburg, Virginia [src: 1]. In May 1961 he joined the Freedom Rides, traveling on the Anniston, Alabama leg on the 14th and on the Montgomery-to-Jackson leg on the 24th; over the course of that activism he was arrested some thirty times [src: 1]. After Howard he transferred to the University of Wisconsin-Madison in 1963 for history and sociology, then later took up race relations and sociology at Harvard [src: 1]. He also worked voter registration in Baton Rouge during the early 1960s [src: 1]. His Library of Congress oral history is entry 27 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1].

sources.title: Dion Diamond oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress (AFC 2010/039)
